checks.a_analysis: The output text accurately reflects the headings and checklist items found in the source PDF across all three pages. The addition of table headers like 'Field' and 'Value', and the boolean 'false' strings used to represent checkbox states, are interpreted as structural normalization for a semantically structured document rather than fabricated text content.

The document contains placeholders for contact information (Name, Title, Email, Phone) and a reference to the 'NC GIS Contact App'. All contact-related fields in the JSON are correctly left empty or correspond to labels in the source; no specific phone numbers, emails, or URLs have been fabricated.

All three pages of the source document are represented in the output JSON. Page 1 content is in the first section, Page 2 in the second, and Page 3 in the third. While some repetitive decorative images (logos and QR codes) present in the source are absent from the third section, the primary informational content is fully preserved.

The contact information table on page 2 of the source document contains a header row followed by 7 empty data rows. The corresponding table in the output JSON (element index 17) only includes a header row followed by 5 data rows. This reduction of approximately 28% in the row count for this data table is a significant omission.
checks.b_evidence_source: The table on Page 2 with headers 'Name', 'Title', 'Email', 'Phone' contains 7 empty rows.
checks.b_evidence_output: The table element at index 17 has 5 data rows following the header row.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: All core text content, including every checklist item and section heading from the source PDF, has been captured in the output JSON. There are no instances of substantial text loss within any given section.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The wording of all checklist tasks and information fields matches the source exactly. Structural changes, such as representing checkboxes as boolean values in a table, are considered acceptable reformatting in a semantic conversion context.

checks.a_analysis: The output preserves the document content across three pages without erroneous duplication. While the document header ('North Carolina Local Government Committee...') is repeated at the top of each page in the output (Section 1 and Section 2), this reflects the intentional repetition in the source document as part of the page-level headers and is not considered a defect.

The output maintains a logical heading hierarchy. H1 is used for the primary committee name, with H2 for main sections ('Staff Training', 'Documentation & Planning', etc.) and H3 for sub-tasks where appropriate. There are no skipped levels or nonsensical nesting. Adjustments between heading levels and paragraphs for the header sub-text across different pages are acceptable normalization.

The 'External Coordination & Partnerships Contacts' table on Page 2 is structurally corrupted. The source PDF contains a header row followed by six empty rows for data entry. The JSON output (element 17) only contains a total of six rows (one header and five data rows), effectively omitting one data entry row. Additionally, checklist sections throughout pages 2 and 3 are converted into two-column tables, which is a structural mismatch from the list-based organization in the source.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 2 contact table contains 1 header row and 6 empty data rows.
checks.b_evidence_output: The rows array for Section 2 element 17 contains only 6 total rows (1 header, 5 data).

checks.a_analysis: The reading order follows the top-to-bottom, left-to-right flow of the original document. Elements such as the general info table, checklist items, contact tables, and documentation sections appear in the same sequence as the source PDF.

While lists on Page 1 are correctly identified as 'list' elements, all subsequent checklists on Page 2 and Page 3 (including 'Documentation & Planning', 'Technology & Infrastructure', 'Data Management & Quality', and 'Emergency Response Tools & Maps') are converted into table structures. This incorrectly transforms the list semantics into tabular key-value pairs (Field/Value), which is a failure in list conversion fidelity.
checks.b_evidence_source: Documentation & Planning (and others) are structured as checklists (unordered lists) in the source.
checks.b_evidence_output: The 'Documentation & Planning' section (element 19) is structured as a table.

checks.a_analysis: The text content of the document is preserved correctly. While the link to 'NC GIS Contact App' on Page 1 is represented as text rather than a functional link object, there are no fabricated or broken links introduced. Minor omissions of link metadata are acceptable.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the logical flow and organization of the source PDF. The multi-page checklist is converted into a continuous single-column layout suitable for the web, grouping items by their original section headers (Staff Training, External Coordination, etc.). The metadata fields at the beginning are appropriately placed in a 'General Information' table. The overall visual organization matches the intent of the original document.

All tables are correctly rendered. The 'General Information' table at the top, the 'External Coordination & Partnerships Contacts' table (4 columns: Name, Title, Email, Phone), and the various checklist tables (Field/Value columns) are visually clear with distinct headers and readable content. There are no overlapping cells or broken borders.

Meaningful images from the source are present in the HTML. The organizational logos (HurriUp, LGC) and the QR codes found in the footers are rendered correctly. Decorative elements like background page borders are excluded as per accessibility best practices.

There is no genuine body content duplicated in the HTML. While the document titles and header logos repeat, this corresponds to the start of each new page in the source PDF (pages 1, 2, and 3), where these elements were present in the original headers. As per the instructions, document titles and headings appearing in navigation/body contexts are not considered duplication. All checklist items and table data are unique to their respective sections, matching the source.
